speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Hannah Morris. Today is 26 of September 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Richard Rozen in Melbourne, Institute of Victoria, Australia. Can you tell us your name please, and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Richard Rozen. R-O-Z-E-N. It used to be Rozencwajg. But I've shortened it. R-O-Z-E-N-C-W-A-J-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Richard only.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15th of April, 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland, Radom, about 100 kilometers from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. So perhaps we'll-- we'll start with talking about your childhood. What-- what do you remember from your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: The good times, or the bad times? Well, the good times is before the war. And not all that much, actually. Life was good and we had a nice house. My father was a surgeon. My mother was a mother-- mothercraft nurse. And I was well looked after. I had nice friends, and probably the one thing that I always remember-- two things I always remember. My father was always too busy to see me. And he was there as a threat whenever I did something wrong and I got my socks dirty or something, or put my finger into something I shouldn't do. Especially when I went into his surgery once and I broke something. [CHUCKLES] So I got a whack with his stethoscope. I remember that, so it was always a threat. Your father will come home and he was going to give you a good smacking. And so I always remember that, but very little. We didn't see him very much. He hardly ever, ever went on holidays with us. My mother took me to various places, and to Warsaw and other places, shopping into the big cities and so on. But my father was always too busy, too busy. I had a little girlfriend I remember very well, Helenka. She was my favorite friend. She was also a daughter of one of the doctors that my father was friendly with. And we were the only two that had pedaling cars in-- before the war in-- in Radom, because they came from France. And we used to pedal our cars, meet at a certain corner. She was the same age as I am, and we used to get petrol into our pedaling car. We were pretending, of course. And we got there and got our petrol and we'd pedal around the city, so. And that was before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I had an auntie that was a few years older than I, well, 10 years older than I, who had just finished her high school before the war. And no, just my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family traditional? Did you celebrate Jewish holidays? Did you go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes and no. My father came from a religious family, but he went to France to study for eight years, and that all came to an end. He didn't sort of worry too much about religion. Tradition, yes, was observed. My mother tried her best, and she-- we used to go to the synagogue of course, on high holidays. My father claimed to have been exempted being a-- being a doctor. He-- he was looking after patients. And so tradition, yes, but that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any holidays, how it was celebrated at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a number of times we'd go to my grandparents. And I-- I cannot recall my father coming too often. For various holidays, we'd go to the grandparents, the one grandparent or the other grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- oh, it was hard to-- well, I remember them, the-- the surname, Blicher. My mother family was Blichers. I-- I can't remember the names anymore. Was-- was just [NON-ENGLISH] type of things. The last time I saw them was in 1939, so I was five years old. But I remember going to holidays to [INAUDIBLE] to-- to my grandparents' places. But not at our house, because there's only three of us and my father was always a bit too busy for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any-- any of the celebrations, how they celebrated it? [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's-- there were other-- there were other children. There were lots of people. There were a lot of people. There was always a-- people used to come in, even friends, families they had. My grandparents from my father's side had a rather religious family. They kept some religious family, so they went on-- they just went on forever. And I used to get bored. And we used to run around. And on certain occasions we used to look for [NON-ENGLISH] and get certain rewards and things like that. And I was never old enough. And I didn't know Hebrew, because I was still under the age of five, or Yiddish, which most of it was in Yiddish in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so I never-- at home? Polish. Always Polish. But the grandparents insisted on talking Yiddish to me. One set of grandparents, my father's, they spoke Yiddish at home. That was a traditional Jewish home, and Polish was a foreign language to them. And so that was basically what I remember of those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember if your parents were involved in Jewish community in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, my mother went to a-- they both went to Jewish high school, so they-- but not really, no. Medical, yes. I mean, Jewish otherwise, but I can't recall really them having much great involvement in-- in Jewish community as such. But through hospital and through both of them being involved in hospitals. I know my mother worked in a Jewish bank before she-- she did nursing, or something like that. That-- that was because there's a photograph in the Radom book and she-- the Jewish Bank, it was called.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that you were playing with your-- you had a best friend, Helenka. Did you play with Jewish children or also with Polish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, combination, yes, Jewish and Polish. She was not Jewish. Helenka was not a Jewish girl, because I remember her well. My parents didn't sort of-- well, my father didn't worry too much about-- he did not make any distinction. I mean, his-- his friends were doctors, and some were Jewish and some were not. And my friends were the children of doctors. Some were Jewish, and some were not. That's the way it worked in Poland in those days, before the war. You sort of classify yourself into a certain class, and-- and that-- that's the class that you were in, so I-- And being not-- non-religious, my father no longer was religious. Then I had no involvement with any-- well, I hadn't been to school or anything like that, or-- or even kindergarten. Was just-- so I had no direct involvement with any Jewish schooling, or any-- anything like that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that you were very little when the war started, but I just wondered if perhaps you might hear some conversations about, from adults, what was happening in Germany with the Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at that time. No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. In 1939, nothing. When the war came in, when they arrived, then of course many things. No. And besides, my parents were the sort of people that would not involve me in any of such conversations. I mean, the things were for adults and things were for children. And that was the way it was. But we weren't as open as we are now with our children. And they have to know everything about life, everything before the-- the age of three. That wasn't the way things were. No. No. No knowledge at all. And nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the war started, were-- were you aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, suddenly there was a lot of commotion. It's happened, well, when the Germans invaded, that was in 1939, whenever, towards the end of the year. And because we're so close to Warsaw, I mean, you could actually hear explosions and all sorts of things. And it was-- things were happening. And everybody started panicking, running and so on. And my father organized a cart, a horse-- horse cart. And everyone seems to be going that way, towards east, east, whoever could. And we-- we joined a-- the march east. And we-- we went on for a few days, and there was explosions still heard far behind us. And we went and went. I think days and nights, maybe two, three days, two, three nights. I can't be sure because my mother has never spoken about this, and no one else knows, because my father perished, of course, during the war. So it's my recollection that-- but days and nights could have been not as long as-- but it seemed like days and nights. We covered something maybe like 200, 300 kilometers, so maybe it wasn't all that many days and too many nights. Until we got across the river Bug, which was separating the German half of Poland to the Russian half of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we actually went east towards the Russian zone. And-- and that's where we-- life settled again to-- to some normality, but different to what it was before. My father still worked as a doctor, but he was now a military doctor, military hospital, the Russian military hospital. And I didn't see much of him either then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were more people, in the house. There were more people than we here. And we just had a big large house for just the three of us. Here there were more people. There were other families, I remember. Another friend of his was there. He was a dentist, and his wife. Then my auntie came with her boyfriend, and my father married them, because they weren't married. And she was rather young, I think, 20 or something. And they were going to Russia, and so my father married them, because I don't think there was a rabbi available in that-- at that particular time in that particular town. And in those days, no rabbi, doctor is the next best. So he married them in a traditional Jewish way. And I was present at the wedding, of course. And then they went away. And life was much too busy for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place that you stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Luboml. Luboml. It was on the other side of the river Bug, just on the other side. Luboml. And-- but life became much too busy. My regular friends and other things have all disappeared, of course. They weren't with me. I couldn't take my car, which was a very upsetting-- upset me, because every day I used to drive in my car. And that went. And other toys arrived, but too-- became too busy. Too many people were coming and going. The house was always busy. It was too much. Too many people, too. And then we suddenly heard that the-- that the Germans were invading. And there was once again a lot of shooting, and lots of-- and the German just went through very quickly to the town. They just-- Russians went. Russians disappeared, and the German followed them. And I could see all that happening through our windows. We were all in hiding. They weren't shooting. There was no shooting at all. They were shooting before, and once they crossed, they just kept on going, and going, going, going. They were going for hours and hours. Seems like days and nights. Kept going and going. And while this was happening, my father made arrangements for us to escape. But it was too late for us to go to Russia proper. So we went into hiding into a village somewhere with some farmers, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe how it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? The hiding part, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, how did you escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to escape. Well, we weren't prisoners at that stage. It was just the Germans were invading, and they were going. But we knew exactly what was happening now, because that was a year after-- a year and a half after the-- they invaded Poland originally. So we knew that there was ghetto, Warsaw ghetto, because my uncle and auntie escaped from-- from the Warsaw ghetto. Told us that their parents were in a very bad way, grandparents. Had brought the last photographs of them. At that stage were still all alive, but they were struggling in the ghetto. And we-- so we knew all that was happening, so when the Germans invaded, my father had no problem understanding that this is what was going to happen to us. Although he didn't expect the invasion. No one did. There was an arrangement, and we're happy to be on the Russian side. And subsequently we-- we went into-- were taken by some-- also by cart horse, and there was some arrangement was made. And we finished up in a farm in an attic, hidden in a cupboard about this size. A meter high, about more than two meters long, and about a meter wide. Because I had no problem walking between my parents. They couldn't walk. They could only crouch. There wasn't high enough-- they could sit and walk, but I could walk between them. So we got-- and that cabinet was behind a false wall. It was a commercial arrangement. My father was buying like rent, running to [INAUDIBLE]. We didn't see the people that would come in, because no one even in the farm knew that we were there in hiding. And we spent the next 13 months in that-- in that cupboard, the three of us, my father, mother, and I, in total darkness. We've never been out of the cupboard. But we used to get food every night, always in the middle of the night. And I could never see the faces of the people that came in, because they had the lantern sort of hidden. So could just see the light. And they would bring us two buckets. One of them was water for-- for drinking. One of them was for toiletry. And because we-- our toilet was in the cupboard. Everything was in the cupboard. And food, always cold. Some, you know, soup, always potatoes, vegetables, very little meat, but bread. Food every day. We ate every day, no problem. And I washed every day with a rag, because we didn't sort of undress. We stayed in the same clothes for 13 months. Yeah. So must've forgotten my pajamas or something. But-- so we-- we washed. And we exercised. And my mother was always crying. She was very upset about the situation. She didn't like being there. My father was quite good. I-- for-- for the first time, I've seen a lot of my father, because until then, my first six, seven years was he was too busy. But now he's in the cupboard, he wasn't so busy anymore. So he used to tell me lots of stories, pretending to be reading. I did my first year at school was in the cupboard. He taught me how to write and read on the palm of my hand, which I thought was good fun. And life for me became very normal. It was actually quite exciting, because he had me going all sort of exercises, and there was lessons in-- in-- in writing and reading and numerical.

speaker: interviewer
text: All in-- all-- all without speaking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All-- all-- well, whispering. Whispering into my ear, because we had to be quiet. And most of the lessons were during the night. So we slept during the day. Have to be quiet during the day. And when the night everybody was asleep, then we used to wash and clean, when they come up with water, and so on. Eat. And then the lesson started through the night. And they stopped in the morning. And I still remember today, if I see a number three, I can feel it on my palm, you know, because it's-- you know, this was the first visual thing. And the letters. Every letter, I know what the letter feels like. C. I know the-- how this-- before I see what a C looks like, I feel the C. And it was good. It was good fun. It actually did me good, because memory. That improved my memory. I'm not suggesting this is the best thing for memory. I've been lucky in-- as years went by, and have been-- had some reasonable success in chess and bridge. And I think it comes from there, you know, from this schooling. It's not the conventional schooling, but it was quite OK. I had no complaints about that. The-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: --money run out, and they kicked us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for you, hearing your mother crying, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother all the time was upset, but I couldn't understand why she was upset. But women cry, so it's OK. My father explained it to me. That's-- big-- big boys don't cry, and I was seven years old, so I stopped crying young. And so we-- we got kicked out. The money run out. We got kicked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the place that the farm was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Everything was secret. I don't think my-- my mother-- well, my mother has never spoken. She's still alive, in a nursing home. She is 92 years old, not in all her capacities, but nevertheless. But she has never spoken about it. It stopped in '39. It starts in '45. And that's it. Blank. If her life depended on it, she would not say a single thing about the war. She didn't have it all that easy. Anyhow, once we got kicked out of the cupboard, we started wandering around the forest. Were given a bundle of food. We started wandering around the forest. Was dark, night. And suddenly the most fascinating things that ever happened to me in my life, and I've had a 61-year-old life, the light suddenly-- something happened. There was a crack somewhere down there. I could see something happening. Now, I hadn't seen light for just over a year, which is a quarter of my life. And it seems like a long time to be in the darkness, as you probably know. A year is a long time in-- in anyone's life, but in a child life it's even longer. Suddenly the light started coming out. And I was getting excited, started jumping up. And then I think I wee'd myself. And my father told me to put my hands on my-- on my eyes, because I will go blind. But, of course, even then I was already crook. I've been crook ever since, you know. I'm a-- I'm a-- cannot be trusted, I should say. I'm not a thief, but I can't be trusted, as they-- people say.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would slip my eye-- my-- my fingers, and-- and I would see that sunrise. It was fantastic. It was such a fascinating thing, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing came up and got bigger and bigger and bigger. And then my father told me that I can look now, slowly, slowly, slowly. But, of course, I was looking all the time, for 10 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- I fooled him all right, didn't I? So that was a way of expressing oneself. And suddenly the light came on, and after a little while, the party stopped, because some couple of Ukrainian guards found us in the forest. We're trying to hide in-- under bushes, and so had no chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started talking, you know, about-- to my parents, you know that we're Jews, Jews. My father is no, no we're not. We're just-- we're-- we're-- he's try to pretend that he-- we're-- we're-- we're not Jewish. And so he had no papers. He had beard. And both my-- my parents were hunchbacked then. They looked a bit like the monkeys I used to go and see at-- in the zoo, because they hadn't straightened up for 13 months. Took them for a while. My mother never recovered. She's always had the-- had a-- her back was permanently this sort of bent, humped. And my father, well, didn't live long enough to recover, so. And as for me, no problem. I-- I was-- I stood up, just started touching the top-- I grew a couple of inches while I was in the cupboard. And got much wiser too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were taken into the interrogation and we were forced to-- we were denied the fact that they were Jewish. I still remember the officer who interrogated us. The Ukrainians took us to a village-- Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and he was in charge. A German officer, first time I've seen-- I was so close to him, I nearly touched his buttons, but my mother pulled my hand away quickly. He stood majestically beautiful, black shiny boots. And we looked all, you know, in rags and dirty, and they hunchbacked. And he looked so magnificent. I-- I wanted to touch him. You know, the buttons were shiny. And maybe-- maybe it was better I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did have some admiration for them, that sort of a nasty breed. Little I knew, but we know what they are now, what they were, those animals. And so he interrogated us to-- to someone. And-- and we denied being Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were asked to take our trousers down. Both my father and I were-- were circumcised. In Poland, only Jews, of course, are circumcised. So there was no problem denying, I mean. If you're circumcised you're Jewish, and that's the end of the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're taken into the detention. And then into the ghetto, Lublin Ghetto, where we spent a few months time there, few months in ghetto. Things were no good in the ghetto at all. No. Where-- where did they put you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was in Lublin, which was close to where we were found. And it was no good at all, because food was very short now. It was no regular food. I didn't count. It already now '43, so children, by then, had-- most of the children had already gone. There weren't too many children left in the ghetto. They had all died or been exterminated. Every morning young bodies were outside-- was winter-- were put outside. Old, old, old people, young people, always naked, because their clothing was a-- a currency. You could buy something over the fence for clothing. And you know, my parents got rid of all their clothing very quickly, get a bit of bread from the Poles, you know, over the fence. So when someone dies, you take off the clothes, and so on. There's no-- no traditional funeral. They were just-- carts were coming in the morning. And I looked through the window, and I was also given a whack by my father. I was getting up too early. I wasn't allowed to get up, because I could see-- hear the carts, so I would come out. I had to wait till the cart went by, because they're just picking up bodies, and it wasn't good for my-- for my well-being to look at all those corpses being picked up in the mornings. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But where-- where were you accommodated?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a house with hundreds of people in the ghetto. Just strange people. My parents went to work. I was told to-- to-- to always stay inside and hide in the cupboard again. But not a cupboard like that, but a normal cupboard, just to stay out of sight. But I sneaked out and the dogs got onto me. The-- the guards sent dogs to chase me, but lucky enough I was quick enough to get away, and I ducked into-- into a-- a house and-- and I hid. Run up, and I hid under the stairs. And the dogs went away, went by. And then I quietly crawled back into my house, only when it was dark, in the evening, which was a few doors away. My parents were very worried. They thought it got me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: --I wasn't so easy to catch, at least. So I got back into my house, and then I stayed in the cupboard for-- quietly during the days-- for about few months, three, four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody that was supposed to take care of you from the other adults in the-- in the-- No, there was-- no. They all went to work. All the adults went to work. So you were by yourself in--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children were already-- had already died, most of them. Well, there was a few around, but not many, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did your parents work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I can't remember. They both went in. They did some manual work. They were doing manual work. They're coming back with a bit of food. So they were given food for two people. Only people who work get food, so they had to share that with me. So there wasn't much, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the food distributed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. They came-- they brought it back with them. We get a bit of bread, bit of whatever. You know, water, milk, whatever. Not milk, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: --they brought something back. Very little, was always hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were the sanitary conditions there, the Lublin Ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my only venture outside was when the dog chased me. So I don't know what it was like outside. I can't remember. I moved so quickly. The scenery wasn't much interesting. But in the house, well, you've got, I don't know, 60 people living in a house with two toilets, who knows what. I mean we had our turn. People were going in and out. There was no-- we'd wash with rags. It was very basic. Totally different what was before the war. But one gets used to it, you see. It sort of-- you know, it's a progression. You get dehumanized as-- as-- as the war goes on. So everything becomes normal. I mean, the abnormal becomes normal, sort of. That became our turn to-- to get exterminated. We-- we got on the list, and we had to go to Treblinka, which is where we were meant to finish our journey. But it was a stroke of luck that when we were in a holding camp, we went by train also, one of those cattle trains that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you know that you are going to Treblinka? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't. I knew we were going somewhere. But my parents knew, yes, because it was a new camp that was established not far from Lublin. And when people come there, turn them over to Treblinka, that was end. They were told that they're going to-- it's a working camp, but they knew. By then, already, they knew in '43 that we're being-- we're going to get exterminated. But you have no option. You just go along. You get weaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents got weaker by then, and, you know, you're not going to fight. So what? You're going to fight the-- the Third Reich. So we're taken there, and fortunately for us-- How-- how were How-- how-- how were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- by-- by cattle train, yes. By cattle train. But wasn't long. Wasn't one of those that you hear about. Was few hours, no, just-- just a journey. And we were taken into a holding camp. We're lucky, because Treblinka, at that stage, had so many customers that they couldn't-- they couldn't exterminate all of them. They couldn't gas them all and-- and cremate them all in-- so there was a backlog. And we were in the backlog. We were waiting our turn. And while we were waiting our turn, my father was told that they needed doctors in the forest. The Polish partisans needed doctors, because Polish doctors didn't practice in the forest. So Jewish doctors were the only ones that they could get. And so he got in contact with someone and told him that he was a doctor. And they didn't believe him at first, and said, well, you know, take me, and take my wife, and-- so the condition was that if he can't prove that he's a doctor, when-- when they'll take him to the partisans, the three of us will be shot. OK. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did it happen that your father heard about it? Do you remember it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, people told him. You know, it was-- it was a known fact. It's like, you know, like people give you a tip. A horse will win something, or you know, bet-- put your money on that horse to win the Melbourne Cup. Or, you know, things like that. They tell you, look, you know, because they find out that he was a doctor. He was helping. Doctors help people in the-- in the camp. I mean, they knew he was a doctor. And they said that, you know, they need doctors in the partisans, and we might-- we'll that you can get smuggled out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got smuggled out by-- by similar guards, Ukrainian guards, in a cart, drawn cart with clothing, with lots of clothing in a pile of clothing. And we're put underneath the clothing. And we were taken out through the camp. Guards were searching it, and I remember they were prodding with fork.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was all arranged in such a way that they wouldn't hit us, because it was-- everybody was on the take, on those-- the-- the Ukrainian guards knew that there were people being smuggled out for some reason, for money. Well, it was not money. It was essential services. And so once we got out, we went into the forest. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now-- now we stop the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
